frame_label: The Makeup Paradox: Unnatural Yet Expected

examples.text: Bhai mujhe na natural girls hi pasand. Itna contour, filter, lashes karke phir bolti hain confidence ke liye. Simple rehna bhi koi cheez hoti hai.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Frames women who use makeup as artificial and implies their grooming is suspect or performative, while positioning 'natural' femininity as the acceptable standard.

examples.text: Kal Rhea ko dekha bina makeup, bilkul alag lag rahi thi yaar. Insta pe sab makeup ka kamaal hai, real life me itni bhi wow nahi hai.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly treats makeup as deceptive and evaluates a woman's worth by comparing her made-up and unmade-up appearance.

examples.text: I don't think this is controversial: women generally look best with minimal makeup. In corporate settings also, a polished but natural look is more respectable than heavy makeup, which can seem like overcompensation. Taking care of yourself is important, but there should be a limit.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Polices women's appearance through a double bind: they must maintain attractiveness and 'take care' of themselves, but visible effort is framed as excessive or morally suspect.

examples.text: Why do so many girls deny that makeup is basically catfishing? If a guy is shown one face on Instagram and another in person, of course he will feel misled. If you are naturally pretty, fine, otherwise don't blame men for preferring honesty.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly frames women's makeup use as deception toward men and makes female value contingent on being 'naturally pretty.'

examples.text:
Speaker A: Nisha takes one hour getting ready for office every day.
Speaker B: Haan, and then says she does it for herself. Be practical yaar, no one spends that much time unless they want attention.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Dismisses a woman's stated autonomy and assumes grooming must be for external, especially male, attention.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Why did you reject that matrimonial profile? She seemed nice.
Speaker B: Nice hogi, but every photo had so much bridal-style makeup. How to trust what she actually looks like? Shaadi ke liye natural beauty matters.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Treats a woman in the marriage market as an object of visual verification and labels makeup as untrustworthy while still demanding 'beauty.'

examples.text: Hot take: the prettiest women are the ones who don't look like they tried too hard. A little kajal maybe, bas. Natural beauty >>> this full face trend.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Presents appearance policing as a harmless preference while devaluing women whose grooming effort is visible.

examples.text: Girls will spend thousands on makeup and then call it self-expression. Please, if your face changes that much after products and filters, don't act shocked when people call it fake.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Frames makeup as fake and delegitimizes women's self-expression, reinforcing the idea that their appearance is open to public judgment.

examples.text: Before the client meeting, the manager told Priya she should look more presentable, but also 'not overdo the makeup' because it sends the wrong impression.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Illustrates the double bind where a woman is required to enhance her appearance yet penalized if the effort is noticeable.

examples.text: At home, bua said a bride should glow on her wedding day, but when Meenal's makeup trial was done she immediately said, 'itna makeup kyun, ladke walon ko asli chehra bhi dikhna chahiye.'
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Demands bridal beauty while condemning the visible labor required to achieve it, and frames makeup as deceptive in a matrimonial context.

examples.text: There's nothing wrong with enjoying makeup, but office policies should be careful not to shame women who wear none or those who prefer bold looks, as long as everyone is neat and professional.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Discusses makeup and professionalism without policing women into a contradictory beauty standard; explicitly supports autonomy across choices.

examples.text: Makeup skills are honestly impressive. Just because someone likes a natural look doesn't mean others are being deceptive; people dress up for many reasons, not just to attract men.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Superficially similar topic but rejects the deceptive framing and affirms that women's grooming choices need not be for male attention.

examples.text: Bro I honestly prefer natural girls yaar. Itna contour-filter karke pic daalne ka kya point, real life mein bilkul alag lagti hain.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Frames women’s appearance as something to be judged for male preference and implies makeup use is deceptive.

examples.text: Riya ko bolo shaadi dekhne wale aa rahe hain, thoda presentable toh lage. Last time bina makeup aayi thi toh bilkul apna khayal hi nahi rakhti laga.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Polices women’s grooming in a matrimonial context, implying she must maintain attractiveness but in an acceptable way.

examples.text: I’m not against makeup, but I think many women overdo it and then call it self-expression. If you need that much product to look confident at work or on dates, it does come across as hiding something rather than being comfortable in your own skin.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Presents makeup as insecurity/deception and dismisses women’s own reasons for using it.

examples.text: Makeup is basically legal catfishing in many cases. Men are expected to accept the 'glam' look, but after marriage the same woman stops trying and suddenly you see the real face.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly objectifies women, frames makeup as tricking men, and asserts women owe sustained attractiveness after marriage.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Nisha wears a full face to office every day.
Speaker B: Haan, and then says she does it for herself. Honestly if it was really for herself, itna effort kaun karta daily? Thoda simple rehna better hai.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Questions the legitimacy of women doing makeup for themselves and polices acceptable femininity.

examples.text:
Speaker A: How did the bride look?
Speaker B: Nice only, but makeup artist ne over kar diya. Ladki naturally pretty ho toh theek hai, warna itna paint karke sundar dikhna thoda fake lagta hai.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Evaluates the bride primarily as an aesthetic object and draws a misogynistic line between acceptable natural beauty and fake enhancement.

examples.text: Hot take: the prettiest women are the ones who wake up beautiful, not the ones spending 2 hours manufacturing a face for Instagram. Natural beauty always wins.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Romanticizes ‘natural beauty’ while shaming women who visibly invest effort in appearance.

examples.text: Girls will put 5 layers of makeup, use Paris filter, then cry when people say they don't look the same offline. Sorry but if you mislead, don't call it confidence.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Uses deception framing and internalized policing of women’s appearance practices.

examples.text: At the appraisal discussion, the manager said Priya is competent but should look a little more polished for client meetings; not too much makeup of course, just enough to seem presentable.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Imposes a double bind: women must wear enough makeup to satisfy appearance norms but not so much as to be judged.

examples.text: While discussing a match, the aunt said the girl should be naturally fair and graceful, not the type who needs heavy makeup every time guests come over.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Treats women in matrimonial evaluation as aesthetic commodities and contrasts idealized natural beauty with condemned visible effort.

examples.text: There’s nothing wrong with liking a no-makeup look on yourself or your partner. The problem starts only when people shame women either for wearing makeup or for not wearing it.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Discusses makeup preferences without policing women; explicitly rejects the double standard.

examples.text: Makeup is an art form and some people enjoy it, some don't. Saying 'she looks better without makeup' is fine as a personal view, but it shouldn't become a rule for how women have to present themselves.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Superficially similar language about makeup preference, but it avoids objectification and rejects prescribing appearance norms.

examples.text: Bro mujhe na natural girls zyada achhi lagti hain. Itna contour, filter, lipstick karke phir bolte ho confidence. Bina makeup bhi achhi lagna chahiye na.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Frames women as needing to be attractive naturally while devaluing visible grooming effort; sets a double standard around acceptable femininity.

examples.text: Ye Insta wali ladkiyan pura catfish karti hain yaar. Reel me alag, real me alag. Itna makeup kyun agar kuch chhupana nahi hai?
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly casts women's makeup use as deception and implies they owe authentic, naturally attractive appearance.

examples.text: I am not against makeup, but I think many urban women have become too dependent on it. In offices in Mumbai, there is pressure to look polished, sure, yet when someone is genuinely confident it shows without layers of foundation. A little grooming is fine, but if a woman needs a full face daily, it does make you wonder whether she is presenting herself honestly.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Positions women as acceptable only within a narrow grooming range and suggests visible makeup indicates insecurity or dishonesty.

examples.text: Men are criticized for saying this, but makeup absolutely misleads people. In arranged marriage meetings, families should see the girl's real face, not salon work and camera filters. If natural beauty is there, why hide behind products?
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Treats women in matrimonial settings as objects for evaluation and frames makeup as deceptive enhancement for male/family judgment.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Rhea office ke liye thoda makeup karti hai, usme kya hai?
Speaker B: Haan theek hai, bas natural lage toh classy lagta hai. Roz itna ready hona matlab attention bhi toh chahiye hota hai thoda.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Suggests women who visibly groom are seeking attention and endorses a narrow ideal of acceptable, male-approved appearance.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Tu bol raha tha Shruti date pe simple aayi thi?
Speaker B: Haan, aur honestly better tha. Aajkal girls pehle itna makeup laga leti hain ki asli face baad me dikhta hai. Fir ladke bolen toh problem.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly frames makeup as hiding a woman's 'real' face and treats her appearance as something men are entitled to assess for authenticity.

examples.text: Hot take: the prettiest women are the ones who don't look like they tried too hard. A clean, natural face >>> 2 hours of makeup. Elegance can't be painted on.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Romanticizes 'natural' beauty while dismissing women who invest visible effort in appearance, reinforcing the unnatural-yet-expected double bind.

examples.text: Some girls say 'I do makeup for myself' but then post 20 selfies and cry when no one notices. If you're actually pretty, you don't need all that. Sab makeup hai, meet them IRL.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Denies women's stated reasons for makeup, assumes it is for validation, and equates cosmetic use with fakery.

examples.text: During appraisal, the manager told Neha she should look a bit more presentable for client meetings, but not go overboard with makeup because it sends the wrong impression.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Imposes a gendered grooming requirement while simultaneously warning against too much effort, exemplifying the workplace double bind.

examples.text: At a rishta discussion, the aunt said the bride should look fresh and fair on the wedding day, but not like those modern girls with heavy makeup because boys' families want natural beauty.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Reduces the bride to appearance standards for male/family approval and demands enhanced but 'natural' beauty, a classic double bind.

examples.text: There is nothing wrong with enjoying makeup as a hobby, but workplaces should not expect women to wear it to look 'professional.' People should be free to show up bare-faced without being judged as careless.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Discusses makeup and professionalism but rejects gendered appearance policing rather than endorsing it.

examples.text: PSA: stop calling every makeup look 'fake.' Some people like bold eyeliner, some like no makeup, and neither choice is more honest than the other.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Superficially similar topic, but explicitly opposes the deception framing and supports women's autonomy in appearance choices.
